coredata.dc:title: A novel strategy for ratiometric determination of o-phenylenediamine via in-situ fluorogenic reaction and generation of metal nanoparticles
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Jingxiang
Huang, Yongfei
Wang, Jiajia
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel ratiometric strategy for ultra-sensitive detection of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was proposed based on combinatorial reactions of in-situ fluorogenic reaction and in-situ formation of red fluorescent dithiothreitol-copper nanoparticles (DTT-CuNPs). Here, Cu2+ is used both as an oxidant and as a precursor. Dehydroascorbic acid (DHAA) is formed via redox reaction of AA and Cu2+. Then, DHAA reacts with OPD to yield blue fluorescent quinoxaline (OXD) with emission peak at 434 nm through in-situ fluorogenic reaction. Red emitting DTT-CuNPs with emission peak at 666 nm is instantly generated due to the coordination reaction between DTT and the residual Cu2+ which is not consumed by AA. The fluorescence intensity (FI) of OXD at 434 nm is closely relied on the concentration of OPD, which can be used as a response signal for OPD detection. Meanwhile, FI of DTT-CuNPs at 666 nm has no significant change, which can be used as a reference signal for OPD detection. Thus, the ratio (F434/F666) of the Cu2+/AA/DTT sensing system is successfully employed to quantify OPD, exhibiting a wide linear range from 0.2 µM to 60 µM, with LOD of 0.09 µM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: O-phenylenediamine (OPD)
In-situ fluorogenic reaction
Ratiometric

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for ratiometric detection of OPD
Construction ratiometric probe for detection OPD
Detection of OPD in water samples

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The UV–Vis spectra of (a) Cu2+, AA and Cu2++AA and (b) OPD, AA + OPD, Cu2++AA + OPD (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of AA, Cu2+, OPD, Cu2++AA and Cu2++AA + OPD.
(a) Zeta potential of DTT and DTT-CuNPs (b) FT-IR spectra of DTT before and after addition of Cu2+ (c) HRTEM image of DTT-CuNPs with scale of 20 nm (d) Size distribution histogram of DTT-CuNPs.
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of DTT and DTT-CuNPs (inset of the a: 1-DTT, 2-DTT-CuNPs) (b) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of DTT-CuNPs.
Fluorescence emission spectra of Cu2++AA, Cu2++AA + OPD, DTT, Cu2++AA + DTT and Cu2++AA + OPD + DTT.

(a) Impact of reagent order on the fluorescence emission spectra of the sensing system (b) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of Cu2+ and AA dependence of time from 0 min to 15 min at an interval of 1.5 min (c) The time-fluorescence course spectra of DHAA and OPD from 0 min to 20 min (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of the sensor against solution pH from 4.0 to 10.0.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the sensing system towards different concentration of OPD from 0 µM to 60 µM (b) The linear curve of F434/F666 versus OPD concentration over the range 0.2 µM to 60 µM.

(a) Selectivity of the probe towards OPD detection in the presence of various interferents (F and F0 was FI of the probe with and without interferents) (b) Anti-interference ability of the probe for OPD (30 µM) detection with different interferents (60 µM).
Diagram of ratiometric detection OPD by the sensing system of Cu2+/AA/DTT.
Chemical reaction between Cu2+, AA and OPD.
Results for detection of OPD in practical samples.

head.ce:title: A novel strategy for ratiometric determination of o-phenylenediamine via in-situ fluorogenic reaction and generation of metal nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Changzhi University, Changzhi 046011, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Changzhi University, Changzhi 046011, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030006, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030006, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel ratiometric strategy for ultra-sensitive detection of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was proposed based on combinatorial reactions of in-situ fluorogenic reaction and in-situ formation of red fluorescent dithiothreitol-copper nanoparticles (DTT-CuNPs). Here, Cu2+ is used both as an oxidant and as a precursor. Dehydroascorbic acid (DHAA) is formed via redox reaction of AA and Cu2+. Then, DHAA reacts with OPD to yield blue fluorescent quinoxaline (OXD) with emission peak at 434 nm through in-situ fluorogenic reaction. Red emitting DTT-CuNPs with emission peak at 666 nm is instantly generated due to the coordination reaction between DTT and the residual Cu2+ which is not consumed by AA. The fluorescence intensity (FI) of OXD at 434 nm is closely relied on the concentration of OPD, which can be used as a response signal for OPD detection. Meanwhile, FI of DTT-CuNPs at 666 nm has no significant change, which can be used as a reference signal for OPD detection. Thus, the ratio (F434/F666) of the Cu2+/AA/DTT sensing system is successfully employed to quantify OPD, exhibiting a wide linear range from 0.2 µM to 60 µM, with LOD of 0.09 µM.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, construction of fluorescent sensors on the basis of in-situ fluorogenic reactions has sparked significant research interest and displayed the advantages of simplicity and convenience for practical application [1–4]. Among this type strategy, o-phenylenediamine (OPD) has particularly received overwhelming attention in designing versatile fluorescent sensors to detect a vast majority of targets, such as metal ions [5], enzymes [6–10], drugs [11], ascorbic acid (AA) [6,12], and so on. So far, most of these probes were explored based on two types of in-situ fluorogenic reaction of OPD. The first type is that the colorless and non-emissive OPD can be easily oxidized in the presence of various oxidants like H2O2 [13] and metal ions [9,14], thereby producing a yellow fluorescent substance of 2,3-diaminophenazine [15] . For instance, Zhao et al [16] have developed a ratiometric fluorescence probe to sense Ag+ based on GQDs and OPD. And the second type is that OPD can react with dehydroascorbic acid (DHAA) to yield blue emissive quinoxaline fluorophore 3-(1,2-dihydroxyethyl) furo[3,4b]-quinoxaline (OXD) [17–19]. In the second type, AA is firstly converted to DHAA by some oxidants, such as Cu2+, Fe3+, norit, iodone and 2,6-dichloroindoindophenol [7,10], then the resultant DHAA interact with OPD to generate blue emitting OXD. For example, Lv et al [7] constructed yellow fluorescent N-CDs coupling with fluorogenic reaction between Cu2+, AA and OPD to detect alkaline phosphatase (ALP).

Despite so many probes based on the peculiar fluorogenic reaction of OPD have been built up to recognize various analytes, however, constructing fluorescent probe to detect OPD itself is also urgently desirable. In China, it has been blacklisted as one of 14 kinds of environmental pollutants [20]. As a vital chemical intermediate for the fabrication of medicine and pesticides, it has been extensively used for the production of surfactants and fur dyes [21–23]. It can easily enter water via sewage discharge and garbage disposal, thereby posing serious harm to the environment and human body owing to its high toxicity and carcinogenicity [24,25]. To the best of our knowledge, some literatures has reported fluorescence detection of OPD based on the first type of in-situ fluorogenic reaction. For example, Ye et al [26] fabricated sulfur quantum dots (SQDs@CMC) with the addition of Cu2+as sensor materials for ratiometric determination of OPD. A similar method was adopted by Ma et al [22] via using CuNCs in the presence of Cu2+ as senor materials for ratiometric detection of OPD. However, ratiometric detection of OPD only by in-situ fluorescence reaction without the step of synthesizing sensor materials were scarcely reported.

Intrigued by these investigations, herein, we devised Cu2+/AA/DTT as a sensor for ratiometric detection OPD using Cu2+ as dual functions, as illustrated in Schem.1. On one hand, Cu2+ was used as the oxidant for OPD to generate blue emissive OXD. On the other hand, Cu2+ was used as the precursor to in-situ produce red-emitting DTT-CuNPs via highly coordination interaction between Cu2+ and DTT [27,28]. Moreover, two other isomers of OPD including m-phenylenediamine (mPD) and p-phenylenediamine (pPD) are not able to trigger the similar fluorogenic reaction, which is very advantageous to be detected specifically. It opens up a brand new ratiometric sensing platform by coupling in-situ fluorogenic reaction with in-situ generation of luminescent CuNPs. Obviously, utilization of this type sensing methodology is free of synthesizing probe materials (Scheme 1 ).

After mixing of Cu2+ and DTT, DTT-CuNPs can be instantly obtained via one-pot reduction method. After 1.0 mL of 0.1 M DTT solution was mixed with 3.0 mL of 10 mM CuSO4 solution at atmospheric temperature, white precipitation was immediately formed, suggesting the successful formation of DTT-CuNPs. Finally, the resultant DTT-CuNPs was characterized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for ratiometric detection of OPD
ce:section.ce:para: 100 μM CuSO4 solution and 40 μM AA solution were added to the PBS buffer solution (pH = 9.0) for well reaction of 12 min. Then, various concentrations of OPD solutions from 0 μM to 60 μM were added in and reacted at room temperature for 19 min. DTT solution was finally introduced in and diluted to 5.0 mL with ultrapure-water. After incubation of 2 min, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded under excitation wavelength at 330 nm. Thus, the calibration plot was easily carried out by using the fluorescence intensity ratio of F434/F666 versus the concentration of OPD.

In-situ fluorogenic reaction of OPD and AA in the presence of Cu2+ was a two-step procedure, as revealed in Scheme.2 . Firstly, Cu2+ reacted with AA to form DHAA via redox reaction. Secondly, the generated DHAA was consumed by OPD via condensation reaction to yield blue emissive OXD. To investigate the reactions between Cu2+ and AA, Cu2+, AA and OPD, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of Cu2+, AA, Cu2++AA, OPD, AA + OPD and Cu2++AA + OPD were compared (Fig. 1 a and Fig. 1b). Individual Cu2+ and AA showed absorption peaks at about 200 nm and 265 nm respectively. However, when Cu2+ and AA mixed together, the absorption peak of AA at 265 nm decreased notably, indicating the chemical reaction of AA and Cu2+ with the formation of DHAA (Fig. 1a). Subsequently, OPD was introduced into the Cu2++AA system, a new absorption peak at about 340 nm appeared which was belonged to OXD (Fig. 1b). However, this peak was nonexistent in the absence of Cu2+. All these results were in accordance with previous report [7], which strongly indicated that Cu2+ can react with AA to produce DHAA followed by generating OXD via further interaction with OPD. To further study the fluorescence feature of OXD, Fluorescence emission spectra of AA, Cu2+, OPD, Cu2++AA and Cu2++AA + OPD were done in Fig. 1c, and their photograph under a UV light were shown in the inset of Fig. 1c (1-AA, 2-Cu2+, 3-OPD, 4-Cu2++AA, 5-Cu2++AA + OPD). It was obviously to see that only the system of Cu2++AA + OPD showed a strong blue emission peak at about 434 nm, strongly suggesting that this blue fluorescence was originated from the product of OXD. Besides, fluorescence emission spectra of OXD under different excitation wavelength from 290 nm to 340 nm were also tested in Fig.S1, indicating its fluorescence property without excitation wavelength dependence.

Dithiothreitol (DTT) exhibited high affinity for Cu2+ due to the existence of two thiol groups (−SH) at both ends [29]. To further research the reaction of DTT and Cu2+, Cu2+ was added into the DTT solution. White precipitate immediately appeared when they were mixed together, revealing the formation of copper nanoparticles (DTT-CuNPs). To study the interaction of DTT and Cu2+, Zeta potential and FT-IR spectra of DTT in the absence and presence of Cu2+ were compared. As seen in Fig. 2 a, the zeta potential of DTT changed from −12.3 mV to 6.23 mV after adding Cu2+, suggesting electrostatic attraction of DTT and Cu2+. To investigate the mode of bonding between DTT and Cu2+, thereafter, FT-IR spectra of DTT in the absence and presence of Cu2+ were conducted in Fig. 2b. By comparison, the stretching vibration peak of S-H bonding at 2554 cm−1 of DTT disappears after the addition of Cu2+, suggesting the successful formation of new S-Cu bonds. Furthermore, TEM characterization was monitored to observe the morphology of DTT-CuNPs. As seen in Fig. 2c and Fig. 2d, the as-obtained DTT-CuNPs were distributed with mean diameter of 5.6 ± 0.3 nm. All the above results demonstrated the successful synthesis of DTT-CuNPs.

With the aim of studying the optical properties of DTT-CuNPs, the photos of DTT and DTT-CuNPs were taken under the room-light and a 365 nm UV light, as shown in the inset of Fig. 3 a (1-DTT, 2-DTT-CuNPs), respectively. It was interestingly to see that DTT emitted no fluorescence but DTT-CuNPs emitted strong red fluorescence when they were put under the 365 nm UV-lamp. In addition, DTT-CuNPs appeared as white milk precipitate in the room-light. Then, the fluorescence emission spectra of DTT and DTT-CuNPs were also detected in Fig. 3. Obviously, a remarkable near infrared emission peak at 666 nm was observed when excited at 330 nm, with a large stokes shift about 336 nm (Fig. 3b), which is in favour of practical application. In the meantime, fluorescence emission wavelength of DTT-CuNPs maintained unchanged under different excitation wavelengths varying from 300 nm to 360 nm (Fig.S2), suggesting no dependence of emission wavelength on excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction ratiometric probe for detection OPD
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the foregoing investigations, a probe for ratiometric detection of OPD was constructed. As revealed in Schem.1, determination of OPD was a three-step procedure. In the first step, Cu2+ was used as an oxidant to interact with AA for the generation of DHAA. In the second step, OPD was introduced in and reacted with DHAA to produce blue emissive OXD. In the final step, a fixed amount of DTT solution was added in to the Cu2++AA + OPD system. Instantly, red-emitting DTT-CuNPs in-situ generated via reacting with the residual Cu2+ which was not consumed by AA. Since the concentration of Cu2+ and AA were fixed, FI of OXD changed upon addition of OPD, while FI of DTT-CuNPs had no significant change. Thus, a ratiometric strategy for detection of OPD was naturally proposed. To verify the feasibility of this method, fluorescence emission spectra of various reaction system (Cu2++AA, Cu2++AA + OPD, DTT, Cu2++AA + DTT and Cu2++AA + OPD + DTT) were further performed in Fig. 4 . As can be seen, fluorescence emission peak at 666 nm belonged to DTT-CuNPs was observed after the addition of DTT to the Cu2++AA system. When OPD was added to the probe system, the emission peak at 666 nm remained nearly unchanged, but a new emission peak at 434 nm originated from OXD was generated. Therefore, ratiometric detection of OPD can be realized by using 666 nm as the reference signal and 434 nm as the response signal.

Given that OPD has two other isomers of pPD and mPD, the UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of Cu2+ + AA + pPD and Cu2+ + AA + mPD were further explored (Fig. S3a and Fig. S3b). Notably, the absorption peak at 340 nm attributed to OXD appeared neither in the spectrum of Cu2+ + AA + pPD nor in the spectrum of Cu2+ + AA + mPD, which was different from that of Cu2+ + AA + OPD, showing only OPD can react specifically with AA among the three isomers. Additionally, no fluorescence emission peak was observed both in the system of Cu2+ + AA + pPD and Cu2+ + AA + mPD in Fig.S4. In contrast, strong blue emission was produced in the system of Cu2+ + AA + OPD. All these dates strongly demonstrated highly specificity of the reaction between Cu2+, AA and OPD, which was beneficial for selective and sensitive determination of OPD.

To gain the best sensing performance, some critical experimental parameters including order of adding reagent, reaction time, solution pH, concentrations of Cu2+ and AA were optimized before detecting, as shown in Fig. 5 . Reagent addition order had significant impact on sensing work, as described in Fig. 5a. No blue emission was found when Cu2+, DTT, AA and OPD were added in turn. Thus, reagent adding order was determined as Cu2+, AA, OPD and DTT successively, involving three-step reaction. Then, reaction time between Cu2+ and AA, DHAA and OPD, Cu2+ and DTT were successively optimized. Optimal reaction time between Cu2+ and AA were selected to be 12 min according to the UV–Vis absorption spectra dependence of time when Cu2+ and AA were mixed (Fig. 5b). After this, reaction proceeding between the generated DHAA and OPD were explored by time-fluorescence course spectra (Fig. 5c). As can be seen, FI of the OXD was not enhanced any longer after 19 min, indicating the reaction was well-balanced at 19 min. Consequently, 19 min was chosen as the optimal reaction time between DHAA and OPD. Next, DTT was introduced to interact with the residual Cu2+, and their reaction time was optimized to be 2 min. Fig. 4d showed fluorescence emission spectra of the sensing system under different solution pH (4.0–10.0), and the relative ratio of F434/F666 versus pH was plotted in Fig.S5. It was found that the ratio of F434/F666 levelled off when pH was in the range of 4.0–7.0 and increased above 7.0, but reached its maximum up to 9.0. Hence, pH = 9.0 was rationally ensured as the optimal pH. Under the above optimal conditions, concentrations of Cu2+ and AA were further decided according to Fig.S6. F434/F666 was stable when Cu2+ concentration was in the range of 100 µM-120 µM, and F434/F666 barely changed when AA concentration was above 40 µM. Thus, optimal concentrations of Cu2+ and AA were opted to be 100 µM and 40 µM respectively.

Under the best sensing conditions, a stepwise increasing concentration of OPD solutions were added into the sensing system and the relative fluorescence emission spectra were recorded in Fig. 6 a. It was obviously to find that FI at 434 nm increased monotonically along with the increasing concentration of OPD, while the change of FI at 666 nm was neglectful. Thus, the ratio of F434/F666 was employed to analyse OPD and presented a good linear relationship range from 0.2 to 60 µM, as depicted in Fig. 6b. The linear equation was described as: y = 0.12482x + 0.17727 (R2 = 0.993), and the LOD was calculated to be as low as 0.09 µM. Compared with the previously reported methods for OPD detection, This sensor showcased comparable detection limit with other methods, besides, this ratiometric strategy can avoid interference from the external environment via built-in self-calibration, as summarized in Table.S1.

To further assess the selectivity and anti-interference ability of this as-designed sensor, the influence of some common metal ions (K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mn2+ and Al3+), analogues (m-phenylenediamine and p-phenylenediamine), and other potential coexisting organics (citric acid −CA, aniline and nitrobenzene) were studied under the identical experimental conditions, as demonstrated in Fig. 7 . Fig. 7a exhibited fluorescence emission performance of the sensing system in the presence of various interferents and only Cu2+ induced fluorescence enhancement under the same conditions, suggesting excellent selectivity of the sensor. Fig. 7b presented anti-interference ability of the probe against OPD (30 µM) detection in the presence of different coexisting chemicals (60 µM). No notable change of F434/F666 was found in the presence of interferents, proving the sensing system was free from interference and was suitable for practical application to detect OPD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of OPD in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability of the established method for ratiometric sensing OPD was applied in genuine water samples. The practical water samples were collected from the lake water in Zhangze reservoir and the laboratory tap water. Before detecting OPD, each water sample was filtered by a syringe filter (0.22 µm). Subsequently, OPD was determined by the the standard addition method and the results were demonstrated in Table 1 .

In conclusion, a ratiometric fluorimetric sensor for selective detection of OPD was rationally designed via combination of in-situ fluorescence reaction of OPD and in-situ formation of red-emitting CuNPs. Here, Cu2+ played a dual function both as oxidant and as precursor, resulting in formation of DHAA and CuNPs, respectively. The resultant DHAA could undergo condensation reaction with OPD to yield blue fluorescence material of OXD. Thus, ratiometric detection of OPD was simply and conveniently realized. Moreover, a wide linear relationship from 0.2 µM to 60 µM was gained, with LOD of 0.09 µM. Besides, practical application for detection of OPD in water samples suggested the excellent feasibility of this designed method.
Jingxiang Shen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Yongfei Huang: Visualization, Software. Jiajia Wang: Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124671.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Talanta
